current_text: Mapping the space between drainage-based agriculture and rewetting-compatible land use.
The climate problem is spatial. The transition problem is systemic.

This prototype turns a curated peatland rewetting literature review into an exploratory atlas: where international evidence comes from, what transition pathways it supports, and which options might be relevant for South German dairy, forage and biogas regions.
From peatland extent to transition priorities

This prototype is not a general peatland-awareness atlas. It tests how spatial peatland extent, drained organic-soil emissions, national implementation layers and literature evidence can be linked into a transition-priority workflow.
Global Peatland Map 2.0 shows where peat-dominated and peat-in-soil-mosaic areas occur.
Country hotspot data show where drained organic-soil emissions are concentrated.
Germany and Baden-Württemberg layers show how global evidence becomes spatially constrained.
Literature evidence links spatial context to wet use, paludiculture, grazing, biomass and governance options.
A data-visualization and decision-support prototype that connects spatial layers, emissions hotspots, regional implementation constraints and evidence-based transition pathways.
It is not a parcel-level rewetting suitability map, not a substitute for hydrological planning, and not a general educational Mooratlas replica.
Each map layer answers a different question
GPM 2.0 global and Europe images provide spatial context only.
Country-level drained organic-soil emissions identify hotspot countries.
Germany and Baden-Württemberg layers bridge from global evidence to regional planning boundaries.
Literature-derived pathways translate spatial pressure into possible land-use transitions.
The map below is the core narrative.
It moves from global peatland extent and pressure to Germany, Baden-Wuerttemberg and pathway interpretation.
Everything after the map is supporting evidence and exploratory interpretation.
Layer stack: Global Peatland Map 2.0 context and country hotspot layers. All images exported from the same ArcGIS global map frame.
Start with the spatial base layer: peatland extent. This is the area where transition questions can arise.
Total emissions highlight countries where peatland drainage creates large absolute climate pressure.
Emission density shifts the view from large countries to places where pressure is intense relative to area.
Total and density tell different stories.
A hotspot can matter because it is large, intense, or both. The atlas keeps both readings visible.
Europe turns global pressure into policy context.
The European frame links peatland transition to regulation, agricultural land use and implementation capacity.
European peatlands form a shared transition space, but implementation still depends on national and regional settings.
The national frame connects peatland transition to agricultural policy, planning responsibilities and data availability.
The national transition area becomes concrete.
The Thuenen Kulisse translates broad peatland context into mapped organic-soil and moor-related implementation areas.
Different soil contexts imply different pathways.
Moor and soil categories help distinguish where rewetting, wet use, grazing or other transition options may be plausible.
The story reaches regional planning scale.
Baden-Wuerttemberg narrows the national frame to a region where land-use conflicts and practical implementation can be discussed.
BK50 shows the regional peat and wetland soil context.
This layer shows where relevant soil contexts occur. It does not yet classify land use, suitability or priority.
The rest of the page is an evidence explorer.

current_text: Use the following modules as interpretation and prototype support for the main atlas story, not as a second linear narrative.
What do the spatial patterns imply for transition pathways?
These modules translate the map story into pathway hypotheses: which forms of wet use, management change or policy support may fit different contexts.
Spatial pressure only becomes useful when linked to feasible pathways

The atlas now separates context, pressure and implementation layers. This section connects those layers to evidence-informed transition pathways. The matrix is not a prescription; it is a prototype logic for asking which pathways are worth testing where.
Wet grassland use under raised water tables
Biomass production on rewetted peat soils
Raised water table, controlled drainage and partial rewetting
Payments, cooperation models and transition governance
Additional signals help test the atlas story.
Country hotspots, regional evidence and South Germany fit scores are exploratory checks. They support interpretation, but they are not the main storyline.
Evidence explorer: where are drained organic soil emissions concentrated?
A first country-level hotspot layer based on FAO/FAOSTAT-derived drained organic soils data. The layer ranks countries by total CO₂ + N₂O AR5 CO₂-equivalent emissions and by emissions density.
Hover or click a country to inspect total emissions, emissions density and drained organic soils area.
Evidence explorer: cases and transition evidence
Points are approximate visual anchors for study regions, policy nodes and review clusters. They are not exact field-site coordinates.
Region cards show key message, supporting papers and South Germany transfer relevance.
Transition pathways between drained farming and full restoration
South Germany fit: which pathways remain plausible?
Qualitative scores translate literature evidence into a first transfer hypothesis. They are not statistical estimates.
This part documents the prototype status, source limitations and remaining experimental components. It is supporting material, not a finished decision tool.
This Atlas is a literature-driven prototype. It is designed to make evidence, assumptions and transition hypotheses visible, not to replace local hydrological-economic modelling.
Scores are literature-informed expert codings, not statistical estimates. The scale is deliberately simple:
Map points are approximate visual anchors for study regions, national model studies, review clusters and policy nodes. They are not exact field-site coordinates.

South Germany fit is a transfer hypothesis based on international literature. It is not a validated regional model result and must be checked against local hydrology, farm structure, machinery access and markets.

The public repository should contain code, derived metadata and own coding tables. It should not contain copyrighted PDFs, confidential project data, licence-unclear raw rasters or unpublished partner data.

Prototype based on curated literature coding. Scores are qualitative and evidence-map points are approximate visual anchors. Hotspot layers from FAO/FAOSTAT, Global Peatland Map, Natural Earth and Eurostat GISCO are planned for Phase B.
